content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322·5662 
Angus I. Saint-Evens 
City Attorney 
P.O. Box 864 
Willows, CA 
95988 
Dear Mr. Saint-Evens: 
Administrotion 
•• 
Executive/legol 
•• 
322-5660 
322-5901 
August 5, 1983 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Stotements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-83-l72 
Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of Mayor 
James Jacobs of the City of Willows. 
The facts, as stated in 
your letter and during our telephone conversation, are that 
Mayor Jacobs was employed as a policeman for the City of Willows 
from 1972-1979. 
He has worked for the Glenn County Sheriff's 
Department from 1979 until the present. 
Both the City of 
Willows and Glenn County are under contract with, and are 
governed by, the Public Employees Retirement System (PERS). 
Under PERS statutes, there is available a retirement plan 
called the "2% at 50 plan." 
Mayor Jacobs is a member of the 
Glenn County Peace Officers Association (GCPOA) which recently 
approached the County concerning the possible adoption of this 
plan. 
It is anticipated that, in the future, the organization 
will also ask the City to adopt the plan. 
You stated that, if 
the County, or both the County and the City adopt the plan, 
Mayor Jacobs' future retirement benefits will be "greatly 
enhanced."ll 
If the GCPOA approaches the City concerning the adoption of 
the retirement plan, the City Council must approve any decision 
concerning the plan. 
Mayor Jacobs has asked whether he must 
disqualify himself from this decision. 
11 
Mayor Jacobs is currently 34 years old. 
If only the City adopts the plan, Mayor Jacobs will not be 
affected. 


Angus I. Saint-Evens 
August 5, 1983 
Page 2 
DISCUSSION 
Government Code Section 871001/ provides that no public 
official can make, or participate in the making of, government 
decisions if he knows or has reason to know that he has a 
financial interest. 
An official has a "financial interest" in a 
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on: 
* * * 
(c) 
Any source of income 
or more in value . . . received by 
within 12 months prior to the time 
made .... 
(Section 87103.) 
. aggregating $250 
the public official 
when the decision is 
In determining whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a 
decision will have a material financial effect on a source of 
income, consideration is given to whether the effect of the 
decision will be to confer a financial benefit or detriment on 
the official of $100 or more. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b) (3) (A).) 
If the retirement plan in questin was through a private 
business entity, Mayor Jacobs would have to disqualify himself 
from any decision foreseeably having a material effect on it. 
However, because the plan is through a governmental entity, he 
is not required to disqualify himself. 
The reason for this is 
that Section 82030(b) (2) provides that an official's income does 
not include salary received from a local government agency and, 
in the Moore Opinion (3 FPPC Opinions 33, No. 76-074, March 1, 
1977), the Commission held that the term "salary" includes 
retirement benefits. 
I hope that I have clarified this matter for Mayor Jacobs. 
If I can be of any further help, please feel free to contact me 
at (916) 322-5901. 
Very truly yours, 
Jan.~ "-
Janis Shank McLean 
Counsel 
Legal Division 
JSM:plh 
2/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. 


July 5, 1983 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento,California 
95814 
Attn: Legal Division, Miss Janis Shank McLean 
Dear Miss McLean: 
It seems that once again I must call upon your exper-
tise in verifying my present my present belief as to a matter 
involving a potential conflict of interest. 
This request is 
being made on behalf of James Jacobs, Mayor of the City of 
Willows and is being made under direct request of Mayor Jacobs, 
a copy of this request is, of course, going to him at his 
residence. 
The factual background in this matter is as follows: 
Mr. Jacobs has been a council member/mayor since the general 
municipal elections in 1980. 
Since November of 1979, he has 
been employed in various duties as a sergent in the Glenn 
County Sheriff's 
Department. 
He belongs to a local employee 
organization entitled the Glenn County Peace Officers Associ-
ation (GCPOA). 
This organization is primarily for the benefit 
of county public safety employees. 
Glenn County is under con-
tract with and is governed by the Public Employees Retirement 
System. 
Prior to being a police officer with the County of Glenn, 
Mr. Jacobs 
was a police officer with the City of Willows be-
tween Sept. 
,1972 and Nov. 
,1979, 
Under the PERS statutes, there is available (primarily 
to California Highway Patrol Officers) a retirement plan called 
the "2% at 50" retirement. 
This means that a retiring law en-
forcement official, ( this legislation only covers public 
safety officers) may retire at age 50 with a pension by PERS. 
Neither the County of Glenn nor the City of Willows, at this 
time, is currently offering to its employees a" 
at 50" re 
tirement plan. 


Page two 
July 5, 1983 
This year the GCPOA requested from the County of Glenn, 
that the County make them eligible for such a retirement plan. 
In order for the County to enact such, there would have to be 
a renegotiation with PERS for the payment of certain sums to 
bring the current employees into line together with co-payment 
or matching funds by the individual officers. 
The request for 
this year has been dropped by the employee organization. 
Based 
upon my experience in such matters as labor negotiations be-
tween public sector employees, it is my belief that the GCPOA 
will continue on a yearly basis to ask for this retirement 
plan as a benefit for its members. 
This system is a very de-
sirable advantage to public safety officers. 
Although the re-
quest has not been made it may be anticipated that the re-
quest for a 2% at 50 plan will be made to the City of Willows 
during the Meet and Confer sessions this year. 
Additionally, the City Council including the mayor has 
direct responsibility in approval of all negotiations a~d de-
cisions made when coming to an accord or dealing with employee 
organizations under the Meyers-Milias Brown Act (Government 
Code Section 35000 et seq.) 
If the City of ~.Jillows adopts the 112% at 50" plan then 
that alone will not figure in the effect on Mr. Jacobs. How-
ever, 
should the County subsequent to the City or visa versa 
adopt the "2io at 50" plan, the City would be required to make 
up the "arrearages"(for lack of a better word) and it would 
greatly enhance Mr. Jacobs retirement plans in the future. 
I note that Mr. Jacobs is at present 34 years old. 
Although Section 87100 et seq. of the Government Code 
does not specifically address this situation and these facts, 
I believe it is reasonable to assume that Mr. Jacobs would re-
main in his current employment until at least age 50; that ul-
timately perhaps, not during Mr. Jacobs tenure on the council 
or perhaps so the City will opt for an adoption of the "2io at 
50" retirement plan. 
Like\vise, I feel as to the County, it 
is reasonable to assume although there is no suggestion as to 
when it will adopt the "2io at 50" plan. 
Assuming that all of the above factors come together at 
the appropriate or inappropriate time as the case may be viewed 
upon, it is my opinion that should Mr. Jacobs vote in favor of 
the adoption of the 112% at 50" retirement plan that he will be 
in violation of the "Act" as it \vould be reasonably foreseeable 
or substantially likely that it will have a material effect 
upon him. 
This, of course, is not an indepth oplnlon, it is prim-
arily based upon Thorner Opinion (lFPPC S~ction 198 Dec~ 4,1975 ) 
I would appreciate any obs~rvations that you might make as to 
this brief analysis and any comments thereon. 


James Jacobs 
Page three 
July 5, 1983 
I should lastly point out one additional factor that there 
is a possibility that if Mr. Jacobs cannot vote, that the council 
could become deadlocked at two vs two on any pay raises for the 
Public Safety Officers. 
Does such a predicament change the ul-
timate decision as to the validity of his participation. 
If you have any questions regarding the above please 
feel free to contact me at your convenience. I thank you 
in advance for your cooperation and assistance. 
AISEjlla 


P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322.5662 
Angus I. Saint-Evans 
City Attorney 
City of Willows 
P.O. Box 864 
Willows, CA 
95988 
Dear Mr. Saint-Evans: 
Administratian ., Executive/legal 
322.5660 
322·5901 
July 15, 1983 
Re: 
A-83-172 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act 
has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an attorney in the 
Legal Division of the Fair Political Practices Commission. 
If 
you have any questions about your advice request, you may 
contact this attorney directly at 916/322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or unless more information is needed to answer your request, you 
should expect a response within 21 working days. 
BAM:l<.m 
Enclosure 
Very truly yours, 
,,J 
. 
Barbara A. Milman 
Chief, Legal Division

sections.question: If the GCPOA approaches the City concerning the adoption of the retirement plan, the City Council must approve any decision concerning the plan. Mayor Jacobs has asked whether he must disqualify himself from this decision.
sections.conclusion: If the retirement plan in question was through a private business entity, Mayor Jacobs would have to disqualify himself from any decision foreseeably having a material effect on it. However, because the plan is through a governmental entity, he is not required to disqualify himself. The reason for this is that Section 82030(b)(2) provides that an official's income does not include salary received from a local government agency and, in the Moore Opinion (3 FPPC Opinions 33, No. 76-074, March 1, 1977), the Commission held that the term "salary" includes retirement benefits.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 871001/ provides that no public 
official can make, or participate in the making of, government 
decisions if he knows or has reason to know that he has a 
financial interest. 
An official has a "financial interest" in a 
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on:
sections.question_synthetic: Must Mayor Jacobs disqualify himself from voting on a city council decision regarding adoption of a Public Employees Retirement System "2% at 50" retirement plan, given that he would receive materially enhanced future retirement benefits if both the county and city adopt the plan?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mayor Jacobs is not required to disqualify himself because retirement benefits from a governmental entity's retirement plan are excluded from the definition of income under Government Code Section 82030(b)(2), and the FPPC has previously interpreted "salary" to include retirement benefits.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter from City Attorney Saint-Evens (July 5, 1983), an acknowledgment letter (July 15, 1983), and the FPPC advice letter response from Janis Shank McLean (August 5, 1983). All key sections clearly identified. Old format from 1983.

QUESTION: If the GCPOA approaches the City concerning the adoption of the retirement plan, the City Council must approve any decision concerning the plan. Mayor Jacobs has asked whether he must disqualify himself from this decision.

CONCLUSION: If the retirement plan in question was through a private business entity, Mayor Jacobs would have to disqualify himself from any decision foreseeably having a material effect on it. However, because the plan is through a governmental entity, he is not required to disqualify himself. The reason for this is that Section 82030(b)(2) provides that an official's income does not include salary received from a local government agency and, in the Moore Opinion (3 FPPC Opinions 33, No. 76-074, March 1, 1977), the Commission held that the term "salary" includes retirement benefits.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Mayor James Jacobs need not disqualify himself from voting on the City of Willows' potential adoption of a PERS "2% at 50" retirement plan, despite the significant material financial benefit he would receive, because retirement benefits from governmental entities are excluded from the conflict of interest analysis under the Political Reform Act.
